1: Bildad's third speech is the shortest in the cycle (6 verses), suggesting the friends are running out of arguments.
2: Mišrāh wĕpāḥaḏ ʿimmô: 'Dominion and dread belong to him' — mišrāh (dominion, government): the governing authority of God. Paḥaḏ (terror, dread) as the accompanying attribute. ʿōśeh šālôm bimrômāyw: 'he makes peace in his high places' — the divine peacemaking in the heavenly council, which Bildad invokes as contrast to Job's turbulence.
3: Hiš ḵĕsāpāyw yimmāneh: 'Can his armies be numbered?' — gĕdûḏ (troop, army): the heavenly host. The unanswerable rhetorical question establishes divine immensity. ʿal-mî lōʾ-yāqûm ʾôrô: 'upon whom does his light not rise?' — universal divine illumination: no one and nowhere outside divine light.
4: Ûmah-yiṣdaq ʾĕnôš ʾim-ʾēl: 'How can any mortal be declared righteous before God?' — ṣāḏaq (to be righteous, be declared righteous, be in the right) used legally: the formal verdict of acquittal. This is the question of 9:2 raised again by Bildad. Yĕlûd ʾiššāh (born of woman): the same creatureliness-phrase as Job 15:14 — birth conditions moral limitation.
5: Hēn ʿad-yārēaḥ wĕlōʾ yaʾăhîl: 'Even the moon gives no real light before him' — ʾāhal (to shine brightly): the moon's light is dim before divine glory. Kôḵāḇîm lōʾ-zakkû bĕʿênāyw: 'the very stars are not pure in his sight' — zāḵaḵ (to be pure, clear): celestial impurity before divine holiness. The scale of the comparison makes human righteousness seem impossible.
6: ʾAp kî-ʾĕnôš rimmāh: 'How much less a mortal, who is a maggot' — rimmāh (worm, maggot): the decomposition-creature. Ûḇen-ʾāḏām tôlēʿāh: 'and a human being, who is a worm' — tôlēʿāh (worm, crimson-worm): the same word used in Psalms 22:7 ('I am a worm and not a man') — the creature beneath notice. Bildad intends to silence Job with creaturely insignificance; Ps 22 and the incarnation reveal that this is exactly the condition God enters in Christ.
